premises.premise: The coherence length of the superconducting order parameter is defined as $\xi = \sqrt{\frac{\phi_{0}}{3\pi^{2}H_{c2}}}$, where $\phi_{0}$ is the magnetic flux quantum and $H_{c2}$ is the upper critical field.

The Ginzburg–Landau (GL) equation approximates the temperature dependence of the upper critical field $H_{c2}$ for temperatures $T$ close to the critical temperature $T_c$, specifically when $\frac{T_{c}-T}{T_{c}}\ll1$. [44]
The Werthamer–Helfand–Hohenberg (WHH) model approximates the temperature dependence of the upper critical field $H_{c2}$ for all temperatures. [45]

The coherence length $\xi$ is related to the Fermi velocity $v_{F}$ and the critical temperature $T_{c}$ by the BCS relation: $\xi = 0.18 \frac{h v_{F}}{k_{\mathrm{B}} T_{c}}$, where $h$ is Planck's constant and $k_{\mathrm{B}}$ is the Boltzmann constant.

premise.title: Scaling Relation for Upper Critical Field

premises.premise: The upper critical field $H_{c2}$ follows the relation $H_{c2} \sim \left(\frac{T_{c}}{T_{2}}\right)^{2}$, implying a dependence on the square of the critical temperature $T_c$.
premises.assumption: The onset of superconducting order (critical temperature $T_c$) under an applied external magnetic field is determined by the criterion of 90% of the resistance in the metallic state.
The Fermi velocities $v_{F}$ are assumed to be the same in the yttrium hydride ($YH_{9}$) and yttrium deuteride ($YD_{9}$) counterparts.

reasoning.step: The authors perform DC resistance measurements on sample 5 (containing $Im\text{-}3m$ $YH_{6}$ at $P_H = 160$ GPa) and sample 6 (containing $P6_{3}/mmc$ $YD_{9}$ at $P_H = 205$ GPa) under external magnetic fields up to 9 T.
Using the criterion of 90% of the resistance in the metallic state [@assumption-6], the authors determine the dependence of the critical temperature $T_c$ on the applied external magnetic field.

The authors fit the experimental $H_{c2}(T)$ data using the Ginzburg–Landau (GL) equation [@premise-2] and the Werthamer–Helfand–Hohenberg (WHH) model [@premise-3] to extrapolate the upper critical fields at zero temperature, $H_{c2}(0\mathrm{K})$.

step.title: Calculation of Hc2(0) for YH6 and YD9

reasoning.step: From the fits, the authors derive: For $hcp-YD_{9}$: $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{WHH}}(0\mathrm{K})=60\mathrm{T}$ and $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{GL}}(0\mathrm{K})=46\mathrm{T}$. For $bcc-YH_{6}$: $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{WHH}}(0\mathrm{K})=157\mathrm{T}$ and $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{GL}}(0\mathrm{K})=107\mathrm{T}$.

Using the derived $H_{c2}$ values and the definition $\xi = \sqrt{\frac{\phi_{0}}{3\pi^{2}H_{c2}}}$ [@premise-1], the authors calculate the zero-temperature coherence lengths $\xi(0\mathrm{K})$: For $bcc-YH_{6}$: 1.45–1.75 nm. For $hcp-YD_{9}$: 2.3–2.7 nm.

To estimate the upper critical field for $hcp-YH_{9}$, the authors use the relation $H_{c2} \sim \left(\frac{T_{c}}{T_{2}}\right)^{2}$ [@premise-5], combined with the assumption that $YH_{9}$ and $YD_{9}$ share the same Fermi velocities $v_F$ [@assumption-7], to yield an estimated $H_{c2}^{WHH}(0\mathrm{K}) = 120$ T (and $H_{c2}^{GL}(0\mathrm{K}) = 92$ T).

conclusion.title: Upper Critical Fields and Coherence Lengths

inference_unit.conclusion: Based on electrical resistance measurements under magnetic fields up to 9 T, the authors derive the upper critical fields $H_{c2}(0)$ using the Werthamer–Helfand–Hohenberg (WHH) model: - $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{WHH}}(0\mathrm{K}) \approx 157$ T for $Im\text{-}3m$ $YH_{6}$. - $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{WHH}}(0\mathrm{K}) \approx 60$ T for $P6_3/mmc$ $YD_{9}$. - $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{WHH}}(0\mathrm{K})$ is estimated at 120 T for $YH_{9}$ (assuming similar Fermi velocities to $YD_9$). The corresponding Ginzburg-Landau coherence lengths $\xi(0\mathrm{K})$ are calculated to be 1.45–1.75 nm for $YH_{6}$ and 2.3–2.7 nm for $YD_{9}$.
